questions.text: You turn on the tap in your city apartment to drink a glass of water. What invisible journey did that water likely take?
options: It was created entirely inside a municipal water tank.
It was pumped straight from the ocean and desalinated.
It began in a flowing river before passing through complex treatment plants.
It came directly from rainfall caught on the city's rooftops.
questions.explanation: It's easy to forget, but our modern plumbing is invisibly tethered to the nearest riverbank. Our daily survival depends on those rivers staying healthy.

questions.text: If industrial chemicals are dumped into a river 100 kilometers away from your home, how might they still end up on your dinner plate?
options: The chemicals evaporate and fall as toxic rain on your food.
Farmers use river water for irrigation, allowing chemicals to absorb into the soil and crops.
The chemicals only affect fish, not vegetables.
They won't; river water naturally destroys all chemicals after 50 kilometers.
questions.explanation: Rivers feed the farms that feed us. Toxic chemicals don't just stay in the water; they accumulate in the soil and the crops we eat.

questions.text: What is the 'heartbreaking contradiction' in how we treat sacred rivers today?
options: We build temples too far away from the riverbanks.
We worship the river as a Mother, yet we allow raw city sewage to flow directly into her.
We don't have enough festivals celebrating water anymore.
We only worship the rivers in the north, but ignore the ones in the south.
questions.explanation: We have separated our spiritual reverence from our civic responsibility. True Dharma means adapting our rituals and infrastructure so we don't harm the physical water.

questions.text: Many believe a holy river is so powerful it cannot be destroyed. Ecologically speaking, why is this dangerous?
options: Because natural rivers have strict physical limits, and crossing them makes the water toxic regardless of its sacred status.
Because the river will eventually change its course and flood cities.
Because it causes people to stop visiting the river entirely.
Because holy rivers flow much slower than normal rivers.
questions.explanation: A river is a physical system. While its spiritual essence may be eternal, the physical water has limits and can absolutely become too toxic to support life.

resultLabels.title: Beginning the Journey
resultLabels.message: You're just starting to untangle the deep connection between our daily lives, our faith, and our rivers. Keep reading—the real story is just beginning.

resultLabels.title: Connecting the Dots
resultLabels.message: You clearly see how our survival and culture are tied to the water. In the next article, we will see exactly how this vital system breaks down.

resultLabels.title: A True River Guardian
resultLabels.message: Perfect! You deeply understand that practical cleanliness is the highest form of reverence. You are ready to dive into the science of pollution in Article 4.
